1: wayyiqrāʿ ʾet-bigdāyw — "he tore his garments" — the standard mourning rite. wayyitkas bassāq — "covered himself with sackcloth" (śaq, H8242, coarse cloth worn in grief and penitence). wayyābōʾ bêt Yhwh — "went into the house of YHWH" — Hezekiah's response to the Assyrian threat is first liturgical: to the temple, not the war council. The pairing of mourning ritual and temple approach models the appropriate response to existential crisis in the Isaianic framework.
2: Hezekiah sends ʾelyāqîm (household administrator), šebnâ (secretary), and the senior priests (ziqnê hakkohanîm) — a delegation of his court's highest officials — to Isaiah. The phrase mekussîm bassāq — "clothed in sackcloth" — indicates the officials also come in mourning dress. The symbolism of sending the sackcloth-wearing court to the prophet signals dependence on YHWH's word rather than military counsel.
3: yôm ṣārâ wĕtôkēḥâ ûnĕʾāṣâ — "a day of distress, rebuke, and disgrace" — three nouns stacking the severity of the moment. The crucial idiom: bāʾû bānîm ʿad-mašbēr wĕkōaḥ ʾayin lĕlēdâ — "children have come to the point of birth but there is no strength to deliver" — a medical metaphor for crisis at the point of maximum tension with no capacity to resolve it. The labor-without-delivery image is a Hebrew idiom for futile anguish.
4: yišmaʿ Yhwh ʾĕlōheykā — "perhaps YHWH your God will hear" — the tentative hope expressed through the envoys. The key phrase: bĕʿad šĕʾērit hannimṣāʾâ — "on behalf of the remnant that is still found" — the šĕʾērît (remnant) motif that is central to Isaiah's theology from ch. 7 onward. Hezekiah's appeal is not for himself but for the surviving remnant — the theological category that anchors YHWH's covenant faithfulness.
5: Isaiah's initial response comes quickly — the servants arrive, and he immediately gives a word (wayyōmer ʾălêhem Yĕšaʿyāhû). This contrasts with other prophets who require time or ritual preparation. The quickness of Isaiah's response reflects his continuous engagement with YHWH's word — he is already prepared to speak.
6: ʾal-tîrāʾ — "do not be afraid" — the standard oracle of salvation formula (cf. 35:4; 41:10; 43:1). middĕbrê hannaʿărîm — "from the words of the young men/servants" — YHWH characterizes the Assyrian blasphemy as coming from insignificant underlings. The divine perspective reframes the crisis: what appeared as an overwhelming imperial army is dismissed as the bragging of naʿărîm (young men, attendants).
7: hinnî nōtēn bô rûaḥ — "behold, I am putting a spirit in him" — the divine intervention will be internal to Sennacherib, not external military defeat alone. The spirit (rûaḥ) that YHWH puts in him will cause him to hear a rumor (šĕmûʿâ) and return to his land, where he will fall by the sword. The prophetic word is unconditional and specific — both the how (rumor) and the where (his own land) are named.
8: Sennacherib's field commander returns to find the king fighting against Libnah (Libnâ) — a Shephelah city — having already withdrawn from Lachish. The military narrative confirms the geopolitical instability that will make the king vulnerable to rumors and distraction. Isaiah's prophetic word (v.7) is already beginning to unfold through natural events.
9: wayyišmaʿ ʿal-Tîrhāqâ melek-Kûš — "he heard concerning Tirhakah king of Cush" — the Ethiopian pharaoh of the 25th Dynasty who was advancing against Sennacherib. This is the "rumor" of v.7. The military intelligence about Tirhakah forces Sennacherib to send a letter to Hezekiah rather than attacking personally — the shift from army to letter marks a psychological retreat disguised as threat.
10: The letter's argument: ʾal-yaššîʾakā ʾĕlōheykā — "do not let your God deceive you" — a theological attack on Hezekiah's trust in YHWH. The claim is that Sennacherib's pattern of victories proves the futility of any divine protection. The gods of the nations have consistently failed; YHWH will be no different. This is the blasphemy that triggers Hezekiah's temple prayer.
11: The catalogue of Assyrian conquests: the Assyrian king addresses Hezekiah with direct reference to what Assyrian kings (malkê ʾaššûr) did to all lands — hōḥărimûm, "devoted to destruction/utterly destroyed" (ḥāram, H2763, the verb of total destruction/holy war). The claim is that herem (total destruction) has been applied universally and YHWH's Jerusalem will be no exception.
12: The defeated nations named: Gozan (Gôzān), Haran (Ḥārān), Rezeph (Reṣep), and the people of Eden in Telassar (Ṭelʾaśśar) — all Aramean/Mesopotamian kingdoms already absorbed by Assyria. The list is designed to overwhelm with precedent. The rhetorical question mî hên ʾăšer hiṣṣîlûm ("where are the gods who delivered them?") challenges Hezekiah to produce a single counter-example.
13: The catalogue continues with Hamath, Arpad, Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivvah — all cities and regions conquered. The roll-call of defeated gods and nations functions as a liturgy of despair designed to produce surrender. What makes Hezekiah's prayer (vv.15-20) so powerful is that it directly engages this theology of comparative divine power.
14: wayyiqqaḥ Ḥizqiyyāhû ʾet-hassĕpārîm — "Hezekiah took the letters" (sĕpārîm, plural — perhaps multiple documents or a letter and accompanying threats). wayyipprōśēm lipnê Yhwh — "spread them out before YHWH." The physical act of spreading the letter in the temple is unprecedented — Hezekiah literally brings the human document into the divine court, making YHWH the judge of Sennacherib's claims. This is prayer as legal action.
15: The prayer begins with the address formula: Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl yōšēb hakĕrûḇîm — "YHWH of hosts, God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim" — the full cultic title. ʾattâ hûʾ hāʾĕlōhîm lĕḇaddĕkā lĕkol mamlĕkôt hāʾāreṣ — "you alone are God over all kingdoms of the earth." The prayer begins with the claim Sennacherib denied — YHWH's universal sovereignty — stated as direct address to YHWH himself.
16: yōšēb hakĕrûḇîm — "enthroned above the cherubim" — the technical formula for YHWH's presence above the ark (1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2; Ps 80:1). The kĕrûḇîm are the heavenly attendants whose wings form YHWH's throne; they are not the storm/war-deity cherubim of Babylonian iconography but the covenant guardians of YHWH's holiness. Hezekiah appeals to YHWH as the transcendent creator who made heaven and earth (ʿāśîtā ʾet-haššāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ).
17: haṭṭēh Yhwh ʾoznĕkā ûšmaʿ — "incline your ear and hear" — the petitionary formula characteristic of royal psalms (Ps 17:6; 31:2; 86:1). pĕqaḥ Yhwh ʿêneykā ûrʾēh — "open your eyes and see" — the request for divine attention activates the anthropomorphic divine perception imagery. dibĕrê Sannĕḥērîḇ ʾăšer šālaḥ lĕḥārēp ʾĕlōhîm ḥāy — "the words of Sennacherib who sent to taunt the living God" — ḥāy (living) is the decisive contrast with the dead idols of the nations.
18: ʾākēn Yhwh — "it is true, YHWH" — Hezekiah acknowledges the historical facts Sennacherib cited. hḥărîbû malkê ʾaššûr — "the Assyrian kings have laid waste all the lands" — the prayer is not naive. Hezekiah admits the military reality before making the theological argument. This intellectual honesty in prayer is itself a form of faith — praying with eyes open to the real situation.
19: wĕnātĕnû ʾet-ʾĕlōhêhem bāʾēš — "they threw their gods into the fire and destroyed them" — Hezekiah now makes the theological argument: those gods were not gods (kî lōʾ ʾĕlōhîm hēmmâ) but the work of human hands (maʿăśēh yĕdê ʾādām), wood and stone (ʿēṣ wāʾeben). They could be burned because they were merely material objects. This is the Isaianic idol-polemic at its sharpest (cf. 44:9-20).
20: wĕʿattâ Yhwh ʾĕlōhênû hôšîʿēnû miyyādô — "and now, O YHWH our God, save us from his hand" — the petition itself is brief after the long theological setup. The purpose clause is striking: lĕmaʿan yĕdĕʿû kol-mamlĕkôt hāʾāreṣ kî ʾattâ Yhwh lĕḇaddekā — "so that all kingdoms of the earth may know that you alone are YHWH." Salvation is requested not merely for Israel's sake but for YHWH's universal glory — a missionary motivation embedded in a prayer of crisis.
21: Isaiah sends Hezekiah a word — šālaḥ Yĕšaʿyāhû ben-ʾĀmôṣ — confirming that his prayer was heard (yaʿan ʾăšer hitpallaltā, "because you prayed"). The word of YHWH is addressed specifically against (ʿālāyw) Sennacherib. The prayer's success is explicitly linked to its content — Hezekiah prayed, therefore YHWH speaks. The connection between prayer and divine speech is direct and immediate.
22: bĕtûlat bat-ṣiyyôn — "the virgin daughter of Zion" — the personification of Jerusalem as a young woman. lāzâ lēz bāz lēz ʾaḥărêkā nūdâ rōʾšāh bat-yĕrûšālaim — "she despises you, she scorns you; behind your back she wags her head, O daughter of Jerusalem." The image of the humiliated Assyrian king being mocked by the city he could not conquer is one of Isaiah's most vivid rhetorical reversals — the greatest military power taunted by a city-girl.
23: ʾet-mî ḥēraptā ûgiddaptā — "whom have you taunted and reviled?" — the rhetorical question reveals the ultimate target of Sennacherib's words. He has not merely insulted Judah but the Holy One of Israel (ʾet-qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl). The elevation of the conflict from Judah-versus-Assyria to YHWH-versus-Assyria reframes the entire narrative.
24: Sennacherib's arrogance described: cutting down Lebanon's cedars and choice firs (kōrĕtî qômat ʾărāzāyw mibḥar bĕrōšāyw), entering the innermost height (mārôm qiṣṣô), the forest of his Carmel. The cedar-cutting is both military metaphor (cutting down armies) and ecological reality (the Assyrian army consumed massive timber). The self-exaltation of the empire is mirrored in its consumption of creation.
25: ʾănî qartî wĕšātîtî mêm zarîm — "I dug and drank water from strange wells" — the Assyrian king's claim to have conquered waterless wilderness and survived by force of imperial will. wĕʾaḥărîb bĕkap pĕʿāmay kol yĕʾōrê māṣôr — "I will dry up with the soles of my feet all the rivers of Egypt" — the boast reaches cosmic proportions. The drying of rivers was YHWH's act at the Exodus; Sennacherib usurps this divine prerogative.
26: hălōʾ šāmaʿtā — "Have you not heard?" — YHWH speaks, now directly addressing Sennacherib. lĕmērāḥôq ʾōtāh ʿāśîtî — "from long ago I made it (the plan)." Everything Assyria has done — the conquests, the devastation — was YHWH's instrument (ʿēṣōt qedem, "plans of old"). The empire did not act autonomously; it fulfilled YHWH's predetermined purpose. The ʿēṣâ of YHWH (14:26-27; 25:1) governs even its enemies.
27: wĕyōšĕbêhem qĕṣar-yāḏ — "their inhabitants were short of hand" — an idiom for powerlessness/helplessness. The defeated cities were like ʿēśeḇ śādeh ûyereq deshe (grass of the field and green vegetation) — weak plants, not through Assyria's strength but through YHWH's preordained limitation of them. The demolished cities were before dismayed — their devastation was already determined.
28: šibteḵā wĕṣēʾtĕkā ûḇōʾăkā yāḏaʿtî — "your sitting and going out and coming in I know" — YHWH's comprehensive surveillance of Sennacherib. The three pairs (sitting/rising, going out/coming in) together represent the totality of human activity — YHWH monitors all of it. wĕʾēt hithraggĕzĕkā ʾēlay — "and your raging against me" — the surveillance is specifically interested in the rage directed at YHWH.
29: yaʿan hithraggĕzĕkā ʾēlay wĕšaʾănānĕkā ʿālâ bĕʾoznāy — "because your raging against me and your arrogance have come to my ears." The consequence: wĕśamtî ḥaqqî bĕʾapkā ûmaśĕgarî bĕśĕpātêkā — "I will put my hook in your nose and my bit in your lips." The animal-capture imagery (ḥāq, hook through nose; maśgēr, bit in lips — used to control horses and led animals) reduces the conqueror of nations to a led beast. YHWH will return him by the way he came.
30: The sign for Hezekiah: ʾākôl haššānâ hazzōʾt sĕpîaḥ ûbaššānâ haššĕnît śāḥîš — "eat this year what grows by itself, and in the second year what springs from that; but in the third year sow and reap, plant vineyards and eat their fruit." The three-year agricultural restoration sign confirms that YHWH's word to Hezekiah will be verified by the land's recovery — the sign is specifically agricultural, addressing the normal cycle of planting and harvest disrupted by siege.
31: wĕyāspâ pĕlêṭat bêt-yĕhûdâ hannišʾārt šōreš lĕmattâ wĕʿāśâ pĕrî lĕmaʿlâ — "the surviving remnant of the house of Judah will again take root downward and bear fruit upward" — the classic remnant-restoration metaphor combining botanical imagery (root/fruit) with the vertical movement of spiritual vitality. This is the Isaiah 6:13 "holy seed" in its positive form — the remnant as the seedbed of restoration.
32: kî miYĕrûšālaim tēṣēʾ šĕʾērît ûpĕlêṭâ mēhar ṣiyyôn — "for out of Jerusalem a remnant will go forth, and out of Mount Zion a band of survivors" — the city is the source from which the remnant disperses and from which covenant life is renewed. qinʾat Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt taʿăśeh zōʾt — "the zeal of YHWH of hosts will accomplish this" — the same formula as 9:7 (the Davidic ruler's kingdom) — divine zeal (qinʾâ) is the engine of both promises.
33: The unconditional protection oracle for Jerusalem: lōʾ yāḇōʾ ʾel-hāʿîr hazzōʾt — "he will not enter this city." The Assyrian will not shoot an arrow (lōʾ-yidreh šām ḥēṣ), come before it with a shield (lōʾ-yĕqaddĕmennāh māgēn), or cast up a siege ramp (lōʾ-yišpōk ʿāleyhā sōlĕlâ). The three negated military actions specify the complete defeat of the Assyrian siege strategy.
34: baddereḵ ʾăšer yāḇōʾ bāh yāšûḇ — "by the way that he came, by that he will return" — the return formula that matches v.29's hook-and-bit imagery. The direction reversal — advance replaced by retreat on the same road — is the sign of YHWH's control over the campaign's trajectory. YHWH declares this in advance (nĕʾum-Yhwh, oracle of YHWH) to prove that the event is his action, not chance.
35: ûgannôtî ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾt lĕhôšîʿāh — "I will defend this city to save it" — the double motivation: lĕmaʿanî ûlĕmaʿan dāwid ʿabdî — "for my own sake and for the sake of David my servant." The salvation is not based on Hezekiah's righteousness alone but on YHWH's self-commitment (divine honor) and his Davidic covenant (bĕrît dāwid). The two grounds are inseparable: YHWH's name and David's covenant.
36: wayyēṣēʾ malaʾk Yhwh wayyak bĕmaḥănēh ʾaššûr — "the angel of YHWH went out and struck down 185,000 in the Assyrian camp." The single agent, the single night, the massive number — the miracle is narrated with stark economy. wayyaškîmû babbōqer wĕhinnēh kullām pĕgarîm mētîm — "in the morning, they arose and behold, all of them were dead corpses." The contrast between the sleeping soldiers' expectation of victory and the morning discovery of mass death is the narrative climax of the Assyrian crisis.
37: Sennacherib departs — wayyisaʿ wayyēlek wayyāšāb Sannĕḥērîḇ — "he set out, departed, and returned" — and dwelt in Nineveh. The fulfilment of Isaiah's prophecy (v.7: "he will return to his land") is stated without elaboration. The brevity is rhetorical: the great conqueror's retreat is dismissed in three verbs.
38: wayhî hûʾ mištaḥăweh bêt nisrōk ʾĕlōhāyw — "he was worshiping in the house of Nisroch his god" — the final irony: the king who mocked YHWH is killed while worshiping his own god, in his own temple, by his own sons. wĕʾadrammeleḵ wĕśarʾeṣer bānāyw hikkuhû ḇaḥereb — "his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer struck him with the sword." The prophecy (v.7) is completely fulfilled. YHWH's word through Isaiah produced Sennacherib's defeat, retreat, and assassination.
